title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Imagine you run a website and your landing page includes a sign-up form for a weekly newsletter. You’re considering changing the wording of the call-to-action button to see if a different phrase encourages more visitors to subscribe. The current button says “Sign up for our newsletter here!” (let’s call this CTA A). The alternative reads “Stay up to date by signing up!” (CTA B). Which one performs better?
To find out, we run an A/B test. Each visitor to the site is randomly assigned to see either CTA A or CTA B. We then track a simple binary outcome: did they sign up (1) or not (0)? Finally, we compare the sign-up rates between the two groups. Random assignment is the secret sauce here — it helps ensure that any difference we observe can be attributed to the wording itself, rather than to pre-existing differences among the visitors who happened to see each version.
This type of experiment is the foundation of modern, data-driven decision making in product design, marketing, and beyond. Over the rest of this post, we’ll use simulation to explore the statistical ideas that make A/B testing reliable.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Imagine you run a website and your landing page includes a sign-up form for a weekly newsletter. You’re considering changing the wording of the call-to-action button to see if a different phrase encourages more visitors to subscribe. The current button says “Sign up for our newsletter here!” (let’s call this CTA A). The alternative reads “Stay up to date by signing up!” (CTA B). Which one performs better?
To find out, we run an A/B test. Each visitor to the site is randomly assigned to see either CTA A or CTA B. We then track a simple binary outcome: did they sign up (1) or not (0)? Finally, we compare the sign-up rates between the two groups. Random assignment is the secret sauce here — it helps ensure that any difference we observe can be attributed to the wording itself, rather than to pre-existing differences among the visitors who happened to see each version.
This type of experiment is the foundation of modern, data-driven decision making in product design, marketing, and beyond. Over the rest of this post, we’ll use simulation to explore the statistical ideas that make A/B testing reliable.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
section: The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
text:
The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
Each visitor either signs up or does not sign up, so each outcome can be modeled as a Bernoulli random variable. Let \(\pi\) denote the probability that a visitor signs up.
Because the CTA shown to a visitor may affect that probability, we allow the sign-up probability to differ across groups. Let \(\pi_A\) be the probability of sign-up under CTA A and \(\pi_B\) be the probability of sign-up under CTA B.
The parameter of interest is the difference in conversion rates:
\[
\theta = \pi_A - \pi_B
\]
A natural estimator of this quantity is the difference in sample means:
\[
\hat{\theta} = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B
\]
Since each observation is either 0 or 1, each sample mean is simply the sample proportion of visitors who signed up. In other words, \(\bar{X}_A = \hat{\pi}_A\) and \(\bar{X}_B = \hat{\pi}_B\).

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Simulating Data
In a real A/B test, the true conversion probabilities would be unknown. For this simulation exercise, however, it is useful to set those probabilities ourselves so that we can compare our estimates to the truth.
Suppose CTA A has sign-up probability \(\pi_A = 0.22\) and CTA B has sign-up probability \(\pi_B = 0.18\). The true treatment effect is therefore:
\[
\theta = \pi_A - \pi_B = 0.04
\]
The next step is to simulate 1,000 observations from each Bernoulli distribution.









Group
Sample Size
Sample Sign-Up Rate




0
CTA A
1000
0.216


1
CTA B
1000
0.178







The table above shows the observed sign-up rates in our simulated experiment. Notice that the sample rates (22.2% for A and 18.1% for B) are very close to the true probabilities we set, but not exactly equal — that’s sampling variability at work. Even with 1,000 visitors per group, our estimate of the difference is 0.038, which is slightly above the true value of 0.04. In a real A/B test we would never know the truth, so understanding the magnitude of this random fluctuation is essential for interpreting results.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Law of Large Numbers
The Law of Large Numbers states that as the sample size grows, the sample mean converges to the population mean. In our setting, this means that our estimator \(\hat{\theta} = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\) should move closer to the true value of \(\theta\) when enough observations are collected.
To illustrate this idea, we can compute the element-wise difference between the simulated outcomes in groups A and B and then plot the cumulative average of those differences as the sample size increases.









At small sample sizes the running estimate is volatile because each additional observation has a large influence on the average. As the sample grows, those fluctuations become smaller and the estimate settles near the true effect of 0.04.
Why this matters: In practice, the Law of Large Numbers justifies the need for sufficient sample sizes in A/B tests. If you stop an experiment too early, the estimate might swing wildly due to random noise, leading to a premature (and possibly wrong) conclusion. Waiting for a larger sample makes the decision more reliable.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Bootstrap Standard Errors
A point estimate alone is incomplete because it does not show how much uncertainty remains. To quantify precision, we need a standard error and a confidence interval.
The bootstrap is a resampling method that estimates uncertainty directly from the observed data. The basic idea is to repeatedly sample with replacement from the original data, recompute the estimator each time, and then use the variability of those replicated estimates as an estimate of the standard error.









Quantity
Value




0
Point Estimate
0.03800


1
Bootstrap SE
0.01766


2
Analytical SE
0.01777


3
95% CI Lower
0.00339


4
95% CI Upper
0.07261







The bootstrap standard error (0.01763) is almost identical to the analytical standard error (0.01771), which gives us confidence in the bootstrap approach.
From a decision-making perspective, the confidence interval is more informative than the point estimate alone. Here, the 95% CI for \(\theta\) is roughly [0.006, 0.076]. Because the entire interval is above zero, we have fairly strong evidence that CTA A outperforms CTA B. However, the lower bound is close to zero, reminding us that the magnitude of the improvement could be modest. In a real business setting, this interval would help stakeholders decide whether the observed lift is both statistically and practically meaningful.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Central Limit Theorem
The Central Limit Theorem states that even when the underlying data are not Normally distributed, the sampling distribution of the sample mean becomes approximately Normal when the sample size is large. Since \(\hat{\theta}\) is a difference in sample means, the same logic applies here.
To demonstrate this, we repeatedly simulate \(\hat{\theta}\) for four different sample sizes and plot the resulting sampling distributions.









When the sample size is small (n = 25), the histogram of \(\hat{\theta}\) is irregular and even shows some discreteness. As n increases to 500, the distribution becomes smooth and bell-shaped — exactly what the Central Limit Theorem predicts.
Why this matters: The CLT is what allows us to use simple Normal-based confidence intervals and hypothesis tests even when the raw data are just 0s and 1s. Without it, analyzing A/B tests would be far more complicated.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Hypothesis Testing
A hypothesis test evaluates whether the data are sufficiently inconsistent with a null hypothesis. In this case, the null hypothesis is that the two CTAs produce the same sign-up rate:
\[
H_0: \theta = 0
\]
The alternative hypothesis is:
\[
H_1: \theta \neq 0
\]
To test this, we standardize the estimate using its standard error:
\[
z = \frac{\hat{\theta} - 0}{SE(\hat{\theta})}
\]
The logic is straightforward. By the Central Limit Theorem, \(\hat{\theta}\) is approximately Normally distributed in large samples. Under the null, its mean is 0. Dividing by its standard deviation therefore produces a test statistic that is approximately standard Normal, which allows us to compute a p-value.









Quantity
Value




0
Estimated Effect
0.03800


1
Standard Error
0.01777


2
z-statistic
2.13882


3
p-value
0.03245







In this simulation, the estimated effect is 0.038, with a z-statistic of 2.14 and a p-value of 0.032. Since the p-value is below the conventional 0.05 threshold, we reject the null hypothesis.
What this means for the business: Rejecting \(H_0\) suggests that the observed difference is unlikely to be due to random chance alone. From a practical standpoint, this provides statistical justification for concluding that CTA A generates a higher sign-up rate. However, statistical significance does not automatically imply business significance. The estimated lift of 3.8 percentage points might be very valuable for a high-traffic site, but less so for a small one. The decision to implement CTA A permanently should also consider implementation cost and long-term impact.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The T-Test as a Regression
The two-sample comparison above can also be written as a simple linear regression. This matters because the regression framework generalizes easily to richer experimental settings (e.g., adding covariates or interactions).
To do this, we stack the two groups into one dataset. Let \(Y_i\) be the sign-up outcome and let \(D_i\) be an indicator equal to 1 for CTA A and 0 for CTA B. Then we estimate:
\[
Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 D_i + \varepsilon_i
\]
In this model, \(\beta_0\) is the mean of group B, while \(\beta_0 + \beta_1\) is the mean of group A. Therefore, \(\beta_1\) is exactly the difference in means, so \(\hat{\beta}_1 = \hat{\theta}\).









Quantity
Value




0
Coefficient on D
0.03800


1
Standard Error
0.01778


2
t-statistic
2.13775


3
p-value
0.03266







The regression output shows a coefficient of 0.041, exactly matching our earlier point estimate. The standard error (0.01764) and t-statistic (2.324) are nearly identical to the two-sample z-test results. The slight differences arise because OLS uses a pooled variance estimate while our earlier formula allowed separate variances per group.
Why this equivalence is useful: It means you can analyze A/B tests using the same tools you’d use for any other regression problem. If you ever need to control for visitor demographics, time-of-day effects, or other variables, the regression framework handles it seamlessly.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Problem with Peeking
A common mistake in A/B testing is to repeatedly check for significance before the planned sample size has been reached. Under classical frequentist inference, this creates a multiple-testing problem: each additional look at the data is another opportunity to reject the null by chance.
To show this, we simulate a setting in which the null hypothesis is true. We set both sign-up probabilities equal to 0.20, generate 1,000 observations per group, and test for significance after every additional 100 observations. We then repeat that entire process 10,000 times and record whether at least one of the ten interim tests is significant at the 5% level.









Quantity
Value




0
Nominal Type I Error
0.050


1
Empirical Type I Error with Peeking
0.199







A single test at the 5% significance level is designed to have a Type I error rate of 0.05. But once the analyst repeatedly peeks at the accumulating data, the probability of obtaining at least one false positive jumps to around 19% in our simulation.
The takeaway for practitioners: This is why many A/B testing platforms either forbid peeking or require a correction (like sequential testing boundaries). Peeking without adjustment dramatically increases the risk of shipping features that are actually no better than the status quo. If you must monitor experiments continuously, use methods designed for sequential analysis rather than repeatedly applying a fixed-sample test.

title: Replication of Angrist & Lavy (1999)
text:
How much does class size actually affect student learning? This seemingly simple question has proven surprisingly hard to answer. The core problem is endogeneity: schools often assign struggling students to smaller classes, and wealthier districts tend to have both larger schools and better outcomes. A naive comparison of large vs. small classes conflates the effect of class size with these confounding factors.
Angrist and Lavy (1999) solve this identification problem using an elegant natural experiment rooted in a 12th-century rabbinical rule. Maimonides’ Rule, still enforced in Israeli public schools today, caps class size at 40 students. When a grade’s enrollment hits 41, the cohort must be split into two classes; at 81, into three; and so on. This creates sharp, predictable discontinuities in class size as a function of enrollment — discontinuities that have nothing to do with student ability or family background.
The key insight is that a school with 40 students in a grade will have one class of 40, while a school with 41 students will have two classes of about 20. These two schools are likely very similar in every other way, yet experience a dramatic difference in class size driven purely by an administrative rule. Angrist and Lavy use the predicted class size from Maimonides’ Rule as an instrumental variable, allowing them to isolate the causal effect of class size on test scores.
Formally, the predicted class size (instrument) is:
\[f_{sc} = \frac{e_s}{\lfloor (e_s - 1)/40 \rfloor + 1}\]
where \(e_s\) is the beginning-of-year grade enrollment in school \(s\). This paper is a landmark application of the regression-discontinuity (RDD) design in economics.

title: Replication of Angrist & Lavy (1999)
text:
How much does class size actually affect student learning? This seemingly simple question has proven surprisingly hard to answer. The core problem is endogeneity: schools often assign struggling students to smaller classes, and wealthier districts tend to have both larger schools and better outcomes. A naive comparison of large vs. small classes conflates the effect of class size with these confounding factors.
Angrist and Lavy (1999) solve this identification problem using an elegant natural experiment rooted in a 12th-century rabbinical rule. Maimonides’ Rule, still enforced in Israeli public schools today, caps class size at 40 students. When a grade’s enrollment hits 41, the cohort must be split into two classes; at 81, into three; and so on. This creates sharp, predictable discontinuities in class size as a function of enrollment — discontinuities that have nothing to do with student ability or family background.
The key insight is that a school with 40 students in a grade will have one class of 40, while a school with 41 students will have two classes of about 20. These two schools are likely very similar in every other way, yet experience a dramatic difference in class size driven purely by an administrative rule. Angrist and Lavy use the predicted class size from Maimonides’ Rule as an instrumental variable, allowing them to isolate the causal effect of class size on test scores.
Formally, the predicted class size (instrument) is:
\[f_{sc} = \frac{e_s}{\lfloor (e_s - 1)/40 \rfloor + 1}\]
where \(e_s\) is the beginning-of-year grade enrollment in school \(s\). This paper is a landmark application of the regression-discontinuity (RDD) design in economics.

title: Replication of Angrist & Lavy (1999)
text:
Main Analysis

Data
The data cover all Jewish public elementary schools in Israel tested in 1991. The unit of observation is the class. Outcomes are average reading (verbal) and math composite scores (scaled 1–100). The key control variable is tipuach, the percent of students from disadvantaged backgrounds (the PD index).

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as ticker
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf
from IPython.display import display

# Load 4th grade data
df = pd.read_stata("files/final4.dta")

# Fix scores > 100 (coding artifact in original data)
df["avgverb"] = np.where(df["avgverb"] > 100, df["avgverb"] - 100, df["avgverb"])
df["avgmath"] = np.where(df["avgmath"] > 100, df["avgmath"] - 100, df["avgmath"])

# Maimonides' Rule predicted class size
df["func1"] = df["c_size"] / (np.floor((df["c_size"] - 1) / 40) + 1)

# Set scores to missing if no test takers
df.loc[df["verbsize"] == 0, "avgverb"] = np.nan
df.loc[df["mathsize"] == 0, "avgmath"] = np.nan

# Apply sample restrictions (identical to original Stata do-file)
df = df[(df["classize"] > 1) & (df["classize"] < 45) & (df["c_size"] > 5)]
df = df[(df["c_leom"] == 1) & (df["c_pik"] < 3)]

print(f"Sample size: {len(df):,} classes")
print(f"Mean verbal score: {df['avgverb'].mean():.1f}")
print(f"Mean math score:   {df['avgmath'].mean():.1f}")
print(f"Mean class size:   {df['classize'].mean():.1f}")

Sample size: 2,053 classes
Mean verbal score: 72.5
Mean math score:   68.9
Mean class size:   30.3




Figure 1b — Class Size by Enrollment (4th Grade)
This figure replicates Figure 1b from the paper. It plots actual average class size and the Maimonides’ Rule predicted class size against school enrollment. The sawtooth pattern in the dashed line (the rule) and the corresponding drops in actual class size at enrollment multiples of 40 are clearly visible — these discontinuities are the source of identifying variation.

fig_data = (
    df.groupby("c_size")
    .agg(actual=("classize", "mean"), predicted=("func1", "mean"))
    .reset_index()
)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5))

ax.plot(fig_data["c_size"], fig_data["actual"],
        color="black", linewidth=0.9, label="Actual class size")
ax.plot(fig_data["c_size"], fig_data["predicted"],
        color="black", linewidth=0.9, linestyle="--", label="Maimonides Rule")

for y in [20, 25, 30, 35, 40]:
    ax.axhline(y, color="gray", linewidth=0.35, linestyle="--")

ax.set_xlabel("Enrollment count", fontsize=11)
ax.set_ylabel("Class size", fontsize=11)
ax.set_title("Figure 1b. Fourth Grade", fontsize=12)
ax.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
ax.set_xlim(0, 220)
ax.set_ylim(0, 45)
ax.xaxis.set_major_locator(ticker.MultipleLocator(20))
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()








The actual class size closely tracks the Maimonides’ Rule prediction, with clear drops at enrollment = 40, 80, 120, and 160. This confirms the rule has real bite — it is a strong first stage for the IV strategy.


Table II (Columns 7 & 10) — Naive OLS
Before introducing the instrument, we estimate a simple OLS of test scores on actual class size with no controls. Standard errors are clustered by school (schlcode), following Moulton (1986).

def ols_cluster(outcome, regressors, data, cluster):
    cols = [outcome] + regressors + [cluster]
    d = data[cols].dropna()
    formula = outcome + " ~ " + " + ".join(regressors)
    m = smf.ols(formula, data=d).fit(
        cov_type="cluster", cov_kwds={"groups": d[cluster]}
    )
    return m

m_t2c7  = ols_cluster("avgverb", ["classize"], df, "schlcode")
m_t2c10 = ols_cluster("avgmath", ["classize"], df, "schlcode")

rows = []
for label, m, var in [
    ("Col 7: Verbal ~ Class Size", m_t2c7, "classize"),
    ("Col 10: Math ~ Class Size",  m_t2c10, "classize"),
]:
    rows.append({
        "Specification": label,
        "Class Size Coef": f"{m.params[var]:.3f}",
        "Std. Error":      f"({m.bse[var]:.3f})",
        "N":               int(m.nobs),
        "R²":              f"{m.rsquared:.3f}",
    })

t2 = pd.DataFrame(rows).set_index("Specification")
display(t2)








Class Size Coef
Std. Error
N
R²


Specification








Col 7: Verbal ~ Class Size
0.141
(0.035)
2049
0.013


Col 10: Math ~ Class Size
0.221
(0.039)
2049
0.025







Interpretation: Without controls, class size has a positive coefficient — larger classes appear to be associated with higher test scores. This is the selection bias at work: larger schools in Israel tend to be in wealthier urban areas with higher-scoring students. The raw correlation is misleading.


Table III (Panel A, Columns 7, 9, 11) — Reduced-Form RDD
Instead of regressing scores on actual class size, we now regress scores on the Maimonides’ Rule instrument (func1), controlling for PD (tipuach) and optionally enrollment (c_size). This is the reduced-form of the RDD strategy.

m_t3c7  = ols_cluster("avgverb", ["func1", "tipuach"],           df, "schlcode")
m_t3c9  = ols_cluster("avgverb", ["func1", "tipuach", "c_size"], df, "schlcode")
m_t3c11 = ols_cluster("avgmath", ["func1", "tipuach"],           df, "schlcode")

rows = []
for label, m, var in [
    ("Col 7:  Verbal ~ func1 + PD",                m_t3c7,  "func1"),
    ("Col 9:  Verbal ~ func1 + PD + Enrollment",   m_t3c9,  "func1"),
    ("Col 11: Math   ~ func1 + PD",                m_t3c11, "func1"),
]:
    rows.append({
        "Specification": label,
        "func1 Coef": f"{m.params[var]:.3f}",
        "Std. Error": f"({m.bse[var]:.3f})",
        "N":          int(m.nobs),
        "R²":         f"{m.rsquared:.3f}",
    })

t3 = pd.DataFrame(rows).set_index("Specification")
display(t3)








func1 Coef
Std. Error
N
R²


Specification








Col 7:  Verbal ~ func1 + PD
-0.085
(0.031)
2049
0.311


Col 9:  Verbal ~ func1 + PD + Enrollment
-0.089
(0.040)
2049
0.311


Col 11: Math   ~ func1 + PD
0.038
(0.040)
2049
0.203







Interpretation: Once we control for PD and use the exogenous variation from Maimonides’ Rule, the coefficient on func1 becomes negative for reading scores (−0.085 to −0.089). A larger predicted class size — driven solely by the administrative rule — is associated with lower test scores. The effect on math scores is near zero and insignificant for 4th graders. These point estimates match the paper exactly.
Note: Standard errors are clustered by school using HC cluster-robust methods. The original paper applies Moulton (1986) corrections, which may produce slightly different SEs; point estimates are identical.

title: Replication of Angrist & Lavy (1999)
section: Something Additional — Why Does the Sign Flip? Visualizing Bias vs. Causation
text:
Something Additional — Why Does the Sign Flip? Visualizing Bias vs. Causation
The naive OLS and the reduced-form RDD tell completely opposite stories. This section makes that contrast concrete and explains why the RDD estimate is the credible one.

The Sign Flip in Numbers

comparison = pd.DataFrame({
    "Method": ["Naive OLS (Table II)", "Naive OLS (Table II)",
                "Reduced-Form RDD (Table III)", "Reduced-Form RDD (Table III)"],
    "Outcome":    ["Verbal", "Math", "Verbal", "Math"],
    "Regressor":  ["Actual class size"] * 2 + ["Predicted class size (Maimonides)"] * 2,
    "Coefficient": [
        m_t2c7.params["classize"],
        m_t2c10.params["classize"],
        m_t3c7.params["func1"],
        m_t3c11.params["func1"],
    ],
    "Std. Error": [
        m_t2c7.bse["classize"],
        m_t2c10.bse["classize"],
        m_t3c7.bse["func1"],
        m_t3c11.bse["func1"],
    ],
})

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4.5))

colors = ["#c0392b", "#c0392b", "#2980b9", "#2980b9"]
markers = ["o", "s", "o", "s"]
labels_plot = ["OLS Verbal", "OLS Math", "RDD Verbal", "RDD Math"]
x_positions = [0, 1, 3, 4]

for i, (_, row) in enumerate(comparison.iterrows()):
    ax.errorbar(
        x_positions[i], row["Coefficient"],
        yerr=1.96 * row["Std. Error"],
        fmt=markers[i], color=colors[i], markersize=9,
        capsize=5, linewidth=1.8, label=labels_plot[i]
    )

ax.axhline(0, color="black", linewidth=0.8, linestyle="-")
ax.set_xticks([0.5, 3.5])
ax.set_xticklabels(["Naive OLS\n(no controls)", "Reduced-Form RDD\n(+ PD control, Maimonides IV)"],
                   fontsize=10)
ax.set_ylabel("Coefficient on class size measure", fontsize=10)
ax.set_title("Sign Reversal: Naive OLS vs. Reduced-Form RDD (4th Grade)", fontsize=11)
ax.legend(fontsize=9, loc="upper right")
ax.set_xlim(-1, 5.5)
ax.grid(axis="y", alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()










Why the RDD Estimate Is Credible
The sign reversal — from positive (OLS) to negative (RDD) — is not a mystery. It reflects two distinct sources of bias in the naive OLS:
1. Between-school selection bias. Larger schools in Israel are disproportionately located in prosperous urban areas. These schools also tend to have larger class sizes and higher-scoring students. This creates a spurious positive correlation between class size and test scores across schools.
2. Within-school selection bias. Even within the same school, principals may assign weaker students to smaller, remedial classes. This further inflates the OLS coefficient.
The Maimonides’ Rule instrument sidesteps both problems. The rule assigns predicted class size based purely on enrollment thresholds — whether a cohort falls just above or just below a multiple of 40. This threshold is effectively random with respect to student ability or family wealth. A school with 41 students is nearly identical to a school with 39 students in every way except that the former must split into two classes of ~20 while the latter has one class of 39.
By controlling for smooth functions of enrollment (the c_size covariate) and the PD index, and using only the discontinuous variation in func1 as the source of identification, the RDD isolates the causal channel: class size → test scores, with all confounders held fixed.
The result: a ten-student reduction in predicted class size is associated with roughly a 0.85–0.89 point increase in 4th grade reading scores — a credible, causally-identified estimate that the naive OLS would have hidden entirely.
